verses.text: Settled in his cedar palace, David is troubled that the ark still dwells in a tent, and proposes to build God a house. Nathan, sharing his zeal, says go ahead. A tender heart grieves to be better housed than the things of God — though even good intentions must wait on God's word.

Settled in his cedar palace, David is troubled that the ark still dwells in a tent, and proposes to build God a house. Nathan, sharing his zeal, says go ahead. A tender heart grieves to be better housed than the things of God — though even good intentions must wait on God's word.

Settled in his cedar palace, David is troubled that the ark still dwells in a tent, and proposes to build God a house. Nathan, sharing his zeal, says go ahead. A tender heart grieves to be better housed than the things of God — though even good intentions must wait on God's word.

That night God corrects them: in all His years with Israel He never asked for a house of cedar, but moved with them in a tent. God's ways are not bound by our ideas of what honours Him. He does not need our buildings; He was content to dwell among His pilgrim people.

That night God corrects them: in all His years with Israel He never asked for a house of cedar, but moved with them in a tent. God's ways are not bound by our ideas of what honours Him. He does not need our buildings; He was content to dwell among His pilgrim people.

That night God corrects them: in all His years with Israel He never asked for a house of cedar, but moved with them in a tent. God's ways are not bound by our ideas of what honours Him. He does not need our buildings; He was content to dwell among His pilgrim people.

God reminds David where he came from — the sheepfold — and all He has done, then turns the plan around: 'the LORD will make thee an house.' David meant to build God a house; God will build David a dynasty. We can never outgive the God who delights to give.

Here is the great covenant: God will raise up David's seed, establish his kingdom, be a father to him, and 'thy throne shall be established for ever.' It reaches past Solomon to Christ, the Son of David and Son of God, whose reign has no end. God's promise outlasts every throne.

Nathan faithfully tells David every word of the vision, holding nothing back. A true messenger of God delivers the whole counsel given him, neither softening nor adding. What God reveals, His servants are to pass on exactly as it was received.

David goes in and sits before the LORD, overwhelmed: 'Who am I, O Lord GOD, and what is my house, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' The greater the grace, the smaller we feel. Wonder, not pride, is the right response to undeserved promises.

David praises God's uniqueness — 'there is none like thee' — and marvels at the privilege of belonging to His redeemed people, Israel, whom God made His own for ever. Personal mercy widens his eyes to the wonder of belonging to the people of God.

David closes by praying God's own promise back to Him: 'do as thou hast said,' and let David's house be blessed for ever, 'for thou, O Lord GOD, hast spoken it.' This is faith's surest prayer — to ask God to do what He has already promised, and to rest in His word.
